content:
# Numbers Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 159-160 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0119-0120
**Source Images:** `NUM_010_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_010_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses and said:

**2** "Make two trumpets of silver -- you are to make them from hammered work. They will serve you for summoning the congregation and for signaling the camps to set out.

**3** When both trumpets are blown, the entire congregation is to assemble before you at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

**4** If only one trumpet is blown, then the leaders -- the heads of the clans of Israel -- are to assemble before you.

**5** When you sound a war-blast, the camps stationed on the east side are to set out.

**6** When you sound a second war-blast, the camps stationed on the south side are to set out. A war-blast is to be sounded whenever they are to set out.

**7** But when assembling the congregation, you are to blow a long, steady note -- not the war-blast.

**8** The sons of Aaron, the priests, are to blow the trumpets. This is to be a permanent statute for you throughout your generations.

**9** When you go to war in your own land against an enemy who oppresses you, you are to sound the war-blast on the trumpets. Then you will be remembered before YHWH your God, and you will be delivered from your enemies.

**10** On your days of rejoicing -- your appointed festivals and your new moon celebrations -- you are to blow the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over your fellowship offerings. They will serve as a reminder of you before your God. I am YHWH your God."

**11** In the second year, on the twentieth day of the second month, the cloud lifted from above the Tabernacle of the Testimony.

**12** And the Sons of Israel set out on their journeys from the wilderness of Sinai. The cloud came to rest in the wilderness of Paran.

**13** They set out for the first time according to YHWH's command through Moses.

**14** The division under the banner of the camp of Judah set out first, company by company. Over its army was Nahshon son of Amminadab.

**15** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Issachar was Nethanel son of Zuar.

**16** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Zebulun was Eliab son of Helon.

**17** Then the Tabernacle was taken down, and the Sons of Gershon and the Sons of Merari set out, carrying the Tabernacle.

**18** Next the division under the banner of the camp of Reuben set out, company by company. Over its army was Elizur son of Shedeur.

**19** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Simeon was Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.

**20** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Gad was Eliasaph son of Deuel.

**21** Then the Kohathites set out, carrying the holy objects. The Tabernacle would be set up before they arrived.

**22** Next the division under the banner of the camp of Ephraim set out, company by company. Over its army was Elishama son of Ammihud.

**23** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Manasseh was Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.

**24** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Benjamin was Abidan son of Gideoni.

**25** Then the division under the banner of the camp of Dan set out -- serving as the rear guard for all the camps -- company by company. Over its army was Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai.

**26** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Asher was Pagiel son of Ochran.

**27** Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Naphtali was Ahira son of Enan.

**28** This was the marching order of the Sons of Israel, division by division, when they set out.

**29** Moses said to Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite, Moses' father-in-law, "We are setting out for the place of which YHWH said, 'I will give it to you.' Come with us and we will treat you well, for YHWH has spoken good things concerning Israel."

**30** But Hobab said to him, "I will not go. Instead, I will return to my own land and to my own kindred."

**31** Moses said, "Please do not leave us, for you know where we should camp in the wilderness, and you can serve as eyes for us.

**32** If you come with us, then whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you."

**33** So they set out from the mountain of YHWH on a journey of three days, with the ark of the covenant of YHWH traveling ahead of them for those three days to seek out a resting place for them.

**34** The cloud of YHWH was over them by day whenever they set out from camp.

**35** Whenever the ark set out, Moses would say, "Rise up, YHWH! Let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you!"

**36** And when it came to rest, he would say, "Return, YHWH, to the countless thousands of Israel!"

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1-2 -- The silver trumpets** (Heb. *aseh lekha shtei chatzotzrot kesef miqshah ta'aseh otam*): The two silver trumpets (*chatzotzrot*) are distinct from the *shofar* (ram's horn) and from the *shophar ha-yovel* (jubilee horn) that feature elsewhere in the Torah. The *chatzotzrah* is a long, straight, hammered-metal tube -- archaeological and Egyptian artistic parallels confirm this -- a precision instrument of human manufacture, not a natural animal horn. The specification *miqshah* ("hammered work") is the same term used for the construction of the golden lampstand (Exod 25:18, 36), linking the trumpets to the craftsmanship of sacred space. They are not mere noisemakers but liturgical instruments, and their fabrication from silver -- a precious but not the most precious metal -- places them in a specific rank within the Tabernacle's material hierarchy: gold for the most holy objects, silver for instruments of communication, bronze for the structural and utilitarian elements. The number two is functional: the system requires acoustic signaling distinctions (one trumpet vs. two, sustained blast vs. war-blast), and a pair enables these variations. The Leningrad Codex on page 159 presents these opening verses with characteristic clarity in the right-hand column. The Vaticanus (pa_0119, third column continuing) reads *poiēson seautō duo salpingas argureas elatous poiēseis autas* -- "make for yourself two silver trumpets, beaten you shall make them" -- following the Hebrew with precision. The LXX *salpinx* is the standard Greek term for the military trumpet, carrying overtones of Greco-Roman civic and martial signaling that would have resonated powerfully with Hellenistic readers.

**v. 3-7 -- The signaling system** (Heb. *ve-taqe'u bahen -- teruah vs. teqi'ah*): The acoustic system established here distinguishes two fundamental sounds: the *teqi'ah* (a long, sustained blast, from *taqa'*, "to thrust, drive, blow") and the *teruah* (a staccato, alarm-like blast, from *rua'*, "to shout, raise a war-cry"). The *teqi'ah* summons the congregation peacefully; the *teruah* signals military movement. Verse 7 makes the distinction explicit: *u-ve-haqhil et ha-qahal tiq'u ve-lo tari'u* -- "when assembling the congregation, blow [a sustained note] and do not sound the war-blast." The careful differentiation between assembly-call and march-signal reflects a community organized around acoustic communication in a pre-literate, desert environment where visual signals would be limited by terrain and distance. The entire camp of Israel, stretching over a vast area according to the arrangement of chapter 2, needed a sound that carried across open ground. Silver, with its resonant acoustic properties, is the ideal material. The rabbis (Rosh Hashanah 33b-34a) engaged in extensive discussion about the precise nature of the *teruah* -- whether it was a wailing sound (*yelalah*), a groaning sound (*genucha*), or a combination -- and these talmudic debates directly shaped the three-part shofar blowing pattern preserved in synagogue liturgy to this day: *teqi'ah - teruah - teqi'ah*. The Vaticanus uses *sēmasia* ("signal") for *teruah* in some instances and *salpizō* ("trumpet/blast") elsewhere, reflecting the Greek translator's struggle to capture the acoustic specificity of the Hebrew terms.

**v. 5-6 -- The directional marching order** (Heb. *u-teqa'tem teruah ve-nasa'u ha-machanot ha-chonim qedmah... u-teqa'tem teruah shenit ve-nasa'u ha-machanot ha-chonim teimanah*): The first war-blast moves the eastern camps (Judah's division, per chapter 2), and the second moves the southern camps (Reuben's division). The Hebrew text as preserved in the Leningrad Codex mentions only two blasts -- for east and south. The LXX, however, adds a third and fourth blast for the western and northern camps, filling out what appears to be a gap in the MT. The question of whether the MT has suffered haplography (accidental omission through scribal eye-skip) or the LXX has expanded by logical completion is debated. The Vaticanus on pa_0119 does indeed include *kai salpisite sēmasian tritēn* ("and you shall sound a third signal") and a fourth, a reading supported by the Samaritan Pentateuch. My dynamic rendering follows the MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex, which contains only two blasts; but the reader should be aware that the LXX Vorlage likely preserved a fuller text at this point. The directional sequence -- east first, then south -- mirrors the clockwise marching order established in chapter 2: Judah (east), Reuben (south), Ephraim (west), Dan (north).

**v. 8 -- Priestly monopoly on the trumpets** (Heb. *u-venei Aharon ha-kohanim yiq'u ba-chatzotzrot*): The restriction of trumpet-blowing to the Aaronide priests is significant. The shofar could be blown by anyone; the silver trumpets are exclusively priestly instruments. This is because the trumpets function not merely as acoustic signals but as mediatory acts -- they bring Israel to YHWH's attention (v. 9: *ve-nizkartem lifnei YHWH*, "you will be remembered before YHWH") and secure divine intervention. The priest stands at the boundary between human need and divine response, and the trumpet is the instrument of that mediation. The phrase *chuqqat olam le-doroteikhem* ("a permanent statute for your generations") elevates the trumpet ordinance from a wilderness expedient to an enduring institution. In the Second Temple period, silver trumpets were indeed blown by priests -- the relief on the Arch of Titus in Rome famously depicts the spoils of the Jerusalem Temple, including what appear to be two long silver trumpets being carried in triumphal procession, the very instruments prescribed in this verse, seized as war booty in 70 CE.

**v. 9-10 -- War and worship** (Heb. *ve-khi tavo'u milchamah be-artzekhem... u-ve-yom simchatkhem u-ve-mo'adeikhem*): These two verses pair the two functions of the trumpets: military defense (v. 9) and liturgical celebration (v. 10). The verbs used are different: for war, *va-hare'otem* ("sound the war-blast," the *teruah*); for worship, *u-teqa'tem* ("blow," the sustained *teqi'ah*). War requires the urgent, alarm-like sound; worship requires the solemn, sustained note. Yet both produce the same theological result: *zikkaron* ("reminder/memorial") before God. The phrase *ve-nizkartem lifnei YHWH Eloheikhem* ("you will be remembered before YHWH your God") uses the *zakar* vocabulary of covenantal memory -- not that God has forgotten Israel and needs reminding, but that the trumpet-blast activates, as it were, the covenantal commitment YHWH has made to protect and bless. The trumpet is therefore a sacramental instrument: it does not merely produce sound but mediates relationship. The section closes with the divine self-identification formula: *ani YHWH Eloheikhem* ("I am YHWH your God"), the same formula that opens the Decalogue (Exod 20:2). The trumpets section (vv. 1-10) thus constitutes a self-contained legal unit, sealed by the covenant formula. The Vaticanus renders *kai anamnestheisesthe enanti kuriou* ("and you will be brought to remembrance before the Lord"), using the passive -- it is not Israel who remembers but Israel who is remembered, the divine passive that pervades the LXX.

**v. 11-12 -- The departure from Sinai** (Heb. *va-yehi ba-shanah ha-shenit ba-chodesh ha-sheni be-esrim ba-chodesh na'alah he-anan me-al Mishkan ha-edut*): This is one of the most momentous verses in the Torah. Israel has been at Sinai since Exodus 19 -- through the giving of the law, the golden calf crisis, the Tabernacle's construction, and the entire legislative corpus of Leviticus and Numbers 1-10. Now, at last, the cloud lifts. The date is precisely recorded: the twentieth day of the second month of the second year. The phrase *na'alah he-anan* ("the cloud went up/lifted") uses the same verb (*'alah*) that described the cloud's movement in 9:17, now employed for the definitive departure. The cloud's destination -- the wilderness of Paran -- marks the transition from Sinai's sacred mountain to the open desert, from the place of revelation to the place of testing. Paran is the liminal zone between Sinai and the promised land, and it is in Paran that Israel will fail most catastrophically (the spy narrative of chapters 13-14). The Leningrad Codex presents verse 11 at the top of the left column on page 159, with a clear parashah break. The Vaticanus (pa_0120, first column) reads *anebē hē nephelē apo tēs skēnēs tou marturiou* -- "the cloud went up from the tabernacle of the testimony." The phrase *Mishkan ha-edut* ("Tabernacle of the Testimony"), used here rather than the more common *ohel mo'ed* ("Tent of Meeting"), echoes 9:15 and emphasizes continuity: the same Tabernacle over which the cloud settled in 9:15 now sees it lift in 10:11.

**v. 13 -- The first setting-out** (Heb. *va-yis'u ba-rishonah al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh*): The phrase *ba-rishonah* ("for the first time" or "at the first") marks this departure as inaugural -- not just another break in camp but the beginning of the march toward the promised land. The combination *al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh* ("at YHWH's command through Moses") brings together divine authority and prophetic mediation. Every departure in the wilderness journey has these two components: YHWH's initiative and Moses' execution. The cloud lifts (divine signal); Moses gives the order (human mediation). Neither acts alone. This verse functions as a heading for the detailed marching order that follows in verses 14-28.

**v. 14-28 -- The tribal march** (Heb. *va-yissa degel machaneh venei Yehudah ba-rishonah le-tziv'otam*): The marching order follows exactly the arrangement prescribed in chapter 2: Judah's division (east: Judah, Issachar, Zebulun), then Reuben's division (south: Reuben, Simeon, Gad), then Ephraim's division (west: Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin), and finally Dan's division (north: Dan, Asher, Naphtali) serving as rear guard (*me'assef le-khol ha-machanot*, v. 25 -- literally "gatherer for all the camps," meaning the unit that picks up stragglers and guards the rear). The Levitical transport is integrated into the march at two points: the Gershonites and Merarites (who carry the structural components of the Tabernacle -- posts, frames, curtains) march after Judah's division (v. 17), while the Kohathites (who carry the sacred objects -- ark, table, lampstand, altars) march after Reuben's division (v. 21). This arrangement ensures that the Tabernacle's framework arrives and is set up (*ve-heqimu et ha-Mishkan*, v. 21) before the Kohathites arrive with the holy objects -- a logistics of reverence. The sacred vessels are never set on bare ground; the house is always ready before the furnishings arrive. The Leningrad Codex on pages 159-160 presents this section across both pages with the tribal names clearly legible. The Vaticanus on pa_0120 preserves the same order. The name Deuel in verse 20 appears as *Reuel* in some LXX manuscripts (a dalet/resh confusion common in Hebrew transmission), but the Leningrad Codex clearly reads *Deu'el*. I follow the MT reading.

**v. 29-32 -- Moses and Hobab** (Heb. *va-yomer Mosheh le-Chovav ben Re'u'el ha-Midyani choten Mosheh*): This brief narrative is one of the most humanly touching passages in Numbers. Moses addresses Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite, identified as *choten Mosheh* -- a term that can mean "father-in-law" or "brother-in-law" (the root *ch-t-n* refers broadly to in-law relationship). Reuel is identified in Exodus 2:18 as the father of Zipporah, Moses' wife, and elsewhere called Jethro (Exod 3:1, 18:1). The relationship between Reuel, Jethro, and Hobab has generated enormous discussion; the most likely resolution is that Reuel is the grandfather or clan patriarch, Jethro the father-in-law, and Hobab the brother-in-law -- but the terminology is genuinely ambiguous in Hebrew. What is theologically striking is Moses' appeal. He does not command Hobab; he pleads. The promise *ve-hetavnu lakh* ("we will do good to you," v. 29) is echoed by *ve-hayah ha-tov ha-hu asher yeitiv YHWH immanu ve-hetavnu lakh* ("whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you," v. 32) -- the verb *yatav* ("to do good") appears three times, binding the human promise to the divine provision. Moses offers Hobab a share in YHWH's blessing -- not as a theological argument but as a practical inducement. And his second appeal (v. 31) is remarkably pragmatic: *ki al ken yada'ta chanotenu ba-midbar ve-hayita lanu le-einayim* -- "because you know where we should camp in the wilderness, and you can be eyes for us." Moses, who receives divine guidance through the cloud and through direct revelation, nevertheless asks a Midianite nomad for navigational help. This is not a failure of faith but a recognition that divine guidance and human expertise are not mutually exclusive. The cloud indicates the general direction; Hobab's desert knowledge identifies the specific terrain -- water sources, defensible positions, grazing land. Faith and practical wisdom walk together. The text never records Hobab's final answer. Verse 32 ends with Moses' second appeal; verse 33 shifts abruptly to the departure. Judges 1:16 and 4:11 suggest that Hobab's descendants (the Kenites) did ultimately settle in Israel's territory, implying that he accepted -- but the narrator's silence at this point is eloquent. The Vaticanus renders *choten* as *gambros* (which can mean son-in-law, brother-in-law, or father-in-law in Greek), preserving the ambiguity.

**v. 33 -- The ark leads the way** (Heb. *va-yis'u me-har YHWH derekh shloshet yamim va-aron berit YHWH nosea' lifneihem derekh shloshet yamim latur lahem menuchah*): The phrase *har YHWH* ("the mountain of YHWH") occurs here for the first time as a designation for Sinai -- a title more commonly associated with Zion in later texts (Ps 24:3, Isa 2:3). The ark travels *lifneihem* ("before them") for three days, seeking out a resting place (*latur lahem menuchah*). The verb *tur* ("to spy out, explore") is the same verb used for the spies' mission in 13:2 (*latur et ha-aretz*, "to explore the land"). Here the ark itself functions as a scout, ranging ahead of the people to find *menuchah* ("rest, resting place"). The theological image is arresting: YHWH's covenant presence, embodied in the ark, goes ahead of Israel not as a rear guard but as a pathfinder. The *menuchah* sought is not merely a campsite but a foretaste of the ultimate rest -- the same word used for the promised land in Deuteronomy 12:9 (*ki lo batem ad attah el ha-menuchah*, "for you have not yet come to the resting place") and Psalm 95:11 (*im yevo'un el menuchati*, "if they shall enter my rest"). The three-day journey (*derekh shloshet yamim*) is a recurring unit in the Exodus narrative (Exod 3:18, 5:3, 8:23, 15:22) and may represent a standard measure of travel or carry symbolic weight as a complete unit of transition. The Leningrad Codex on page 160 presents this verse clearly. The Vaticanus reads *kataskepsasthai autois anapausin* -- "to spy out rest for them" -- using *kataskeptomai*, the same verb used for military reconnaissance, underscoring the ark's active, purposeful movement.

**v. 34 -- The cloud by day** (Heb. *va-anan YHWH aleihem yomam be-nos'am min ha-machaneh*): This brief verse confirms that the cloud of YHWH's presence was over them during the daytime march. The phrase *be-nos'am min ha-machaneh* ("when they set out from camp") specifies the cloud's protective function during the vulnerable moments of departure -- the times when the camp was dismantled and the people were in motion, exposed and undefended. The cloud is not merely a navigation beacon but a shield. Isaiah 4:5-6 will later develop this image: *u-vara YHWH al kol mekhon har Tziyyon... anan yomam... u-le-masseh u-le-machseh mi-zerem u-mi-matar* -- "YHWH will create over every site of Mount Zion... a cloud by day... and a shelter from storm and rain." The protective cloud is one of the Torah's most enduring images of divine care.

**v. 35-36 -- The ark sayings** (Heb. *va-yehi bin'so'a ha-aron va-yomer Mosheh: qumah YHWH ve-yafutzu oyvekha ve-yanusu mesan'ekha mi-panekha. U-ve-nuchoh yomar: shuvah YHWH rivvot alfei Yisrael*): These two verses are among the most liturgically significant in the entire Torah. In the Leningrad Codex, they are set apart by inverted *nun* letters (called *nun menuzeret* or *nun hafukhah*) before verse 35 and after verse 36, a scribal marking found nowhere else in the Pentateuch. The Talmud (Shabbat 115b-116a) treats these two verses as constituting a separate "book" of the Torah, meaning that Numbers actually contains three books: 1:1-10:34, 10:35-36, and 11:1-end. If so, the Torah contains not five books but seven. Rabbi Shimon ben Gamliel's alternative view is that these verses are "displaced" from their original position (which he identifies as the arrangement of the camp banners in chapter 2), and the inverted *nun* marks bracket them as textually out of place. Either way, the scribal tradition recognized these verses as extraordinary. The first saying -- *qumah YHWH* ("Rise up, YHWH!") -- is a battle cry, calling upon YHWH to arise as a divine warrior scattering his enemies. The verb *yafutzu* ("let them scatter") and *yanusu* ("let them flee") depict not a prolonged battle but an instantaneous rout: YHWH's rising is itself the victory. Psalm 68:2 quotes this verse nearly verbatim (*yaqum Elohim yafutzu oyvav ve-yanusu mesan'av mi-panav*), placing it in a hymnic context of divine triumph. The second saying -- *shuvah YHWH rivvot alfei Yisrael* ("Return, YHWH, to the countless thousands of Israel") -- is an invocation of homecoming. The word *rivvot* ("myriads, ten-thousands") and *alfei* ("thousands") together express the vast multitude of Israel. The verb *shuvah* ("return") implies that when the ark moved forward, YHWH's presence went ahead of the people; when it stopped, YHWH's presence returned to dwell *among* them. The movement of the ark thus enacts a rhythm of divine departure and return, advance and indwelling, that mirrors the theological rhythm of the entire wilderness narrative. These two utterances have been incorporated into Jewish synagogue liturgy: *qumah YHWH* is recited when the Torah scroll is removed from the ark (*aron ha-qodesh*), and *shuvah YHWH* (or a related formula) accompanies its return. The physical Torah scroll, housed in a cabinet called "the ark," reenacts in every service the march of the wilderness ark, and Moses' words echo across three thousand years each time the scroll is lifted and carried through the congregation. The Vaticanus (pa_0120) reads *exegerthēti kurie diaskorpisthētōsan hoi echthroi sou* -- "Rise up, Lord, let your enemies be scattered" -- and for the return: *epistrepson kurie chiliadas muriadōn en tō Israēl* -- "Return, Lord, to the thousands of myriads in Israel," reversing the order of *rivvot* and *alfei* (myriads and thousands) compared to the MT, a minor difference that does not affect the meaning.

**Structural note on chapter 10 in its context**: Numbers 10 divides into three distinct sections, each marking a transition in Israel's life: the trumpet legislation (vv. 1-10), the departure narrative (vv. 11-28), and the human-and-divine guidance unit (vv. 29-36). The trumpet section completes the preparatory legislation begun in chapter 1 -- Israel now has everything needed for organized wilderness life: census, camp arrangement, Levitical assignments, purity provisions, and a communication system. The departure narrative (vv. 11-28) is the fulfillment of everything the first ten chapters have prepared for: Israel finally moves. The Hobab narrative (vv. 29-32) inserts a deeply human moment into the grand military procession -- Moses, the man who speaks with God face to face, asks his in-law for practical help finding water and shade. And the ark sayings (vv. 35-36) provide the theological frame: every departure is an act of divine warfare against chaos, and every arrival is an act of divine homecoming to the people. Chapter 10 is thus both an ending and a beginning -- the conclusion of Israel's Sinai sojourn (which began in Exodus 19) and the inauguration of the wilderness march toward the promised land. What follows in chapter 11 will shatter the orderly beauty of this departure with Israel's first complaint, the fire at Taberah, and the craving for meat -- the wilderness testing begins almost immediately after the wilderness journey does. The contrast between the disciplined, obedient march of chapter 10 and the disintegration of chapter 11 is one of the most dramatic juxtapositions in the Torah.
